questions.specification: One advantage of mass production systems is:

parts.content: Analyse how understanding component parts aids in designing efficient production systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Knowing part complexity informs machine/tool requirements. <marks>1 marks</marks>
- Part geometry affects manufacturing processes like moulding or cutting. <marks>1 marks</marks>
- Identifying tolerances ensures precision in assembly. <marks>1 marks</marks>
- Material properties of parts determine appropriate manufacturing techniques. <marks>1 marks</marks>
- Efficient layout planning depends on the size and interaction of components.<marks>1 marks</marks>
- Reduces waste and cost by aligning design with capabilities. <marks>1 marks</marks>


<note>Award <marks>1 marks</marks> for each relevant link between part understanding and production efficiency up to <marks>6 marks</marks>.</note>

questions.specification: Which production method would be **least** cost-effective for manufacturing a single, complex luxury yacht?

options.markscheme: Job (one-off) production is the most cost-effective method for a single, complex item as it minimizes fixed costs and allows for high levels of customization.
Batch production is more efficient than mass production for low volumes, though it remains less cost-effective than job production for a single unique unit.
Cellular production organizes work into teams; while more flexible than mass production, it is still not the most efficient method for a single, unique yacht.

Mass production involves extremely high fixed costs and specialized equipment designed for high-volume standardized output. For a single unit, the average cost would be prohibitively high, making it the least cost-effective method.

questions.specification: What is a defining characteristic of one-off production systems?

What is one major drawback of the production method used in this setup?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdRioL5UfIqdfAJbX7c0vn1nEWeUo35vNQYVkB-xI3gNnIwz8zJ0NjE2_45xg3UfRobZLvlCD8SYvSjBiEbIB-ugROeYwTjMEXLWW0LPKWwx9T7agoPy-vYT8RP0MpckZnW2-PS9Q?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
Which type of production system is best suited for highly customized, low-volume products?

Which category best fits this system based on the level of human-machine interaction?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdp87P1T0D8MvCla0kIXnIujK-13CRWX_qFjbBeUBozAPbSLg5CM_SRJFjLzSsjsuKWZ836INreY2c0jPEBZWq0zg11EgvbNsSM9JKlTrxbzB5hEY5SeTjmKtayYm7rEzORTiiVDQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
The decision to use CNC machining instead of manual tools may be influenced by:

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of automation versus mechanization in a modern manufacturing facility.
parts.markscheme:
- State that automation involves computer-controlled systems, whereas mechanization uses human-operated machines <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that automation increases consistency and reduces labour costs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Contrast the flexibility of mechanization with its potential for introducing human error <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify that automation suits large-scale production, while mechanization suits smaller, varied tasks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compare the cost structures, noting that automation has higher initial costs but greater long-term savings <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describe how mechanization typically involves lower setup costs and training needs <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Limiting production to injection moulding affects design by:

What aspect of this production system most affects the final product’s function?
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a581b5d5-457e-4618-9cea-3c6df8e16e52)
In which scenario is continuous flow production most suitable?

A product design consultancy is investigating how different joining methods impact assembly speed, product longevity, and aesthetic quality across various manufacturing scenarios. To present their findings, they gather examples from a range of industrial and small-scale production settings.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** depicts a fast, automated joining process used in high-volume vehicle manufacturing.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/yrwZGLg-lgwHPVVu1fRf4)

Identify the joining method used in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Spot welding <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the limitations of spot welding when used in design projects requiring modularity or future disassembly (**Figure 1**).
parts.markscheme:
- Spot welding creates permanent joints that cannot be reversed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes repair, modification, or recycling more difficult <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Requires expensive equipment and trained operators <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High heat input may distort thin materials or affect finishes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a manual fastening method commonly used in consumer-ready DIY furniture kits.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/KoH5kHha0GV45LMAieqDl)

Outline two reasons why screws are commonly used in flat-pack furniture (**Figure 2**).
parts.markscheme:
- Easy for consumers to assemble without special tools <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allow furniture to be disassembled and reassembled <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a low-cost adhesive technique used in small-scale, short-run or temporary assemblies.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/VoDwXwYhq-6BfS7iBld2N)

Explain three benefits of using hot glue for packaging tasks (**Figure 3**).
parts.markscheme:
- Quick setting time enables rapid assembly in small-scale production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Adheres well to porous materials like cardboard <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Requires minimal setup and training, reducing costs <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the durability and reusability of the joints in **Figures 2 and 3**.
parts.markscheme:
- Screws (Figure 2) allow repeated disassembly without damaging parts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hot glue (Figure 3) forms a semi-permanent bond, less reusable <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Screws are more durable under mechanical stress <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hot glue may weaken with heat or humidity <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how the three joining methods shown affect product life span, aesthetics, and production scalability.
parts.markscheme:
- Spot welding offers high strength and scalability but lacks aesthetic appeal without finishing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Screws support maintenance and modularity but may be visually intrusive <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Hot glue is visually clean and easy to apply but has low long-term durability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Screws allow scalable assembly in both manual and semi-automated systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Spot welding is ideal for high-volume robotic automation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Glue bonding is best for temporary or low-stress products <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which factor is **LEAST** likely to influence the choice of a manufacturing technique?
Which aspect is **LEAST** relevant to designing a production system?

options.markscheme: Transportation routes (external logistics) are primarily a concern of distribution and facility location rather than the internal design, layout, and process flow of the production system itself.
Manufacturing limitations (e.g., equipment capabilities, technical constraints) are fundamental to designing a production system as they determine what can be produced and how.
Product decomposition involves breaking down a product into its constituent parts to determine the assembly sequence, which is essential for designing production line stages.
Understanding how components interact is vital for determining assembly methods, quality control points, and the overall configuration of the production system.

questions.specification:
## Question
Based on the image, which best describes the production system shown?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeQ_v7RKdH3LXN4F06j4HGPhXfG45W6doa4I2m6ZEEG8C7hbVduuMSDwVGyV6ccAbXqS4WA1mXq52_XDKC594196Q8FUYI1ej-43Jkc5kaebTKHTNsdcYJ2KGXmYFIG7vW9tIEvDQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

parts.content: Describe how the scale of production influences the choice between batch and continuous production.
parts.markscheme:
* **States** that batch production is more suitable for medium volumes with variation. <marks>A1</marks>
* **States** that continuous production is ideal for large-scale manufacturing with minimal variation. <marks>A1</marks>
* **Describes** how batch production allows flexibility between product runs. <marks>A1</marks>
* **Describes** how continuous production is cost-efficient for standardised goods. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each clear comparison or effect up to [4].</note>

questions.specification: A team of product designers is tasked with developing a new reusable water bottle. They must evaluate the appropriate production system and techniques, keeping in mind cost, scale, and sustainability. The questions below explore how different production systems affect decisions made during design and manufacture.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A simplified diagram of a craftsperson manually assembling wooden toys

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/eZgoo3X959DLd92qV54M2)

Refer to **Figure 1**. State one characteristic of a craft production system.
parts.markscheme:
It relies heavily on skilled human labour rather than machines. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For correctly identifying a defining feature of craft production.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: Image of an automated robotic assembly line for car manufacturing

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/-KvaeIChwXCk41Iy6PgTy)

Refer to **Figure 2**. Outline two advantages of automation in a production system.
parts.markscheme:
Increased precision and consistency in product quality. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Lower long-term labour costs and increased production speed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For each valid and clearly stated advantage of automation.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A table comparing batch production and mass production across features such as cost, setup, and flexibility

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/enQothsQTzl0Qreel75Vs)

Refer to **Figure 3**. Compare batch production and mass production in terms of setup cost, production flexibility, and product uniformity.
parts.markscheme:
Batch production typically has lower setup costs than mass production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Batch production offers more flexibility to produce different products or variants. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Mass production results in higher product uniformity due to continuous automation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For each relevant and distinct comparison between the two production types.*

parts.content: Explain how environmental considerations can influence the choice of a production system.
parts.markscheme:
Designers may choose systems that minimise energy consumption to reduce carbon emissions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Craft or localised production may be preferred to reduce transportation impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Material waste reduction may lead to favouring lean production systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Sustainability goals may encourage selection of modular or disassemblable designs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For each well-explained way that environmental factors affect system selection.*

parts.content: Discuss how the scale of production influences manufacturing technique selection, using examples.
parts.markscheme:
One-off production typically uses manual tools and techniques for custom products like bespoke furniture. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Batch production suits seasonal items like limited edition packaging. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Mass production requires heavy upfront investment in moulds and automation, ideal for items like smartphones. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Continuous production is optimal for high-volume, low-variation items such as plastic bottles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Mass customisation uses flexible manufacturing systems, such as 3D printing for personalised shoes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The cost per unit generally decreases as scale increases due to economies of scale. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For a range of examples with clear explanation linking scale to manufacturing techniques.*

parts.content: Evaluate how limitations of a production system can affect the final design of a product.
parts.markscheme:
Products designed for mass production may require simplified geometry for moulding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Complex handcrafted details may not be feasible in automated systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Available machinery may restrict material selection or joining methods. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Design aesthetics might be compromised to align with system capabilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award: For thoughtful discussion on how constraints shape the product’s design and appeal.*

questions.specification: A manufacturing consultant has been hired to advise several businesses on selecting the most suitable production system for their products. To support their recommendations, they analyse three real-world examples of how different production methods are used across industries, as shown in **Figures 1–3**.

parts.content:
**Figure 1**: A bakery using conveyor ovens to bake identical loaves of bread

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/-hz8-SFv9tvWrrTlBAfj_)

Explain why mass production (**Figure 1**) is well-suited for bread manufacturing.
parts.markscheme:
Bread has a high, predictable demand that justifies continuous production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Standardised loaves allow for minimal variation between units. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Conveyor systems and automation reduce unit cost and labour. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each well-explained point relevant to mass production efficiency, max [3].*

parts.content: Discuss how automation impacts efficiency and cost in mass production (**Figure 1**).
parts.markscheme:
Automation increases production speed by removing human error. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reduces long-term labour costs despite high initial investment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Consistent output improves quality control and reduces waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Can operate continuously, increasing overall throughput. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Requires specialised maintenance and technical oversight. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Might limit adaptability to product changes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each well-explained impact of automation in the context of mass production, max [6].*

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A small business customising T-shirts with names using heat press machines

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/mq_rmTxYG2tKb6v0_d2nL)

Outline two limitations of batch production as seen in **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
Downtime between batches can reduce efficiency. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Reconfiguring equipment for each batch may increase costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per valid limitation related to batch production, max [2].*

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A single engineer assembling a one-off prototype drone

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0Z8wADLPJU1xqyFYeWomm)

Identify the production method shown in **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
One-off production <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for the correct identification of the production type.*

parts.content: Compare the flexibility of the production systems in **Figure 2** and **Figure 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Figure 2 (batch production) allows some variation between batches (e.g., custom names). <marks>1 mark</marks>

Figure 3 (one-off production) offers the highest flexibility, as products are individually crafted. <marks>1 mark</marks>

One-off production can accommodate unique requirements and complex specifications.<marks>1 mark</marks>

Batch production may require tooling adjustments but is still more rigid than one-off. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per clearly explained comparison, max [4].*

questions.specification: Selecting the appropriate production scale ensures:

options.markscheme: Selecting the correct scale of production is intended to improve efficiency, whereas higher labour turnover is generally a negative outcome associated with poor management or diseconomies of scale.
While efficient production can lead to lower prices or better availability, customer satisfaction is a broader objective influenced by many factors beyond just the scale of production.
The scale of production does not directly correlate with the legal compliance or risk management of a firm.
Choosing the appropriate scale of production (such as the minimum efficient scale) allows a firm to achieve productive efficiency, ensuring that resources are used in the most cost-effective way.

questions.specification: Which factor determines the choice of the scale of production for a product?

options.markscheme: Volume of demand is the primary factor that determines whether a product is produced as a one-off, in batches, mass-produced, or through continuous production.
Product size may influence the manufacturing method or facility layout, but the volume of demand determines the scale (quantity) of production.
The number of employees is typically a result of the scale of production and the level of automation, not the initial determining factor for choosing the scale.
Shape complexity influences the specific manufacturing processes used (e.g., casting vs. machining), but not the classification of the scale of production.

questions.specification: Why might a manufacturer choose batch production over mass production?

Which production system is best suited for the product shown in the image?
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ec5de8a2-7dc4-4da2-a15e-6a2b2febc149)

This question explores how the selection and limitations of production systems affect the manufacturing process, product design, and overall efficiency. Answer all questions using the information provided in **Figures 1–3**.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows an example of a continuous production system used to manufacture large quantities of a single product.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5YfZX_pGx7XNVqgIj9kKV)

Define *computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM)* and name one advantage of using it in large-scale production.
parts.markscheme:
- Computer-integrated manufacturing (CIM) is the use of computer systems to control the entire production process. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It allows seamless coordination between design, planning, and manufacturing for greater efficiency. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** illustrates a traditional craft production method often used for small-scale, high-skill manufacturing.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Fum6zuBeeCl0PLhy1pN66)

Using **Figure 2**, explain two limitations of using craft production for high-volume manufacturing.
parts.markscheme:
- Craft production is labour-intensive, limiting scalability for large quantities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inconsistent quality may result from manual processes without automation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Time per unit is higher compared to mechanized methods. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Costs per item are higher due to lack of economies of scale. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award up to 2 marks for each well-explained limitation, up to 4 marks maximum. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Responses must relate to Figure 2. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the sustainability implications of continuous production (**Figure 1**) and craft production (**Figure 2**).
parts.markscheme:
- Continuous production generates more waste but can use more efficient energy systems. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Craft production has lower environmental impact per unit but is slower and less scalable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Continuous systems may optimise material use but require large capital investment. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Craft methods allow use of local and renewable materials. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award 1 mark per well-explained comparison up to 4 marks maximum. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** displays a batch production system incorporating digital fabrication technologies.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/codqcRseTnzxIhbezjoQL)

With reference to **Figure 3**, describe how batch production benefits from digital technologies such as 3D printing.
parts.markscheme:
- Allows rapid production of small customised product runs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Digital files can be easily modified between batches. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces tooling costs by avoiding fixed moulds. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award 1 mark for each relevant point clearly connected to batch production and digital technologies. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how understanding the *component parts* of a product helps in selecting an appropriate production system.
parts.markscheme:
- Complex parts may require precision machinery or automation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Standardised parts favour mass or assembly-line production. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Custom parts might be better suited for batch or craft production. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award 1 mark per clearly explained link between component design and production system selection. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how limitations of production systems can influence the *function* and *aesthetics* of a product, using examples.
parts.markscheme:
- Injection moulding limits product shapes to those that can be ejected from a mould, influencing aesthetics. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNC machining allows high accuracy but may restrict internal geometries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Craft production enables organic forms but lacks consistent quality control. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mass production favours simple, repeatable shapes that reduce visual uniqueness. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitations in finishing techniques can affect texture, gloss, and surface appeal. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Design decisions must account for machine capabilities, which may restrict creativity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award 1 mark per clearly explained link between system limitation and design outcome, up to 6 marks maximum. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A constraint in the design process might arise due to:

What factor most likely influenced the manufacturing method used for this product?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXc2JeL_3PSkDdNgAGJ3MW6JVQXHcmpiIPEyEJ7Jx0y2g-Rw3Xp7m2XSycpXGNhl4yQW3pdNMpNQdhZOIwEkd7AF7KSShWByIkQUwO1bPxcQ36axROi1ME-QlNuUaEAcf0dc8A0JKQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
A well-designed production system should consider:

The following diagram illustrates a specific manufacturing process.

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdU85s1Y0_i8Ej0I0gG_DR6LnHJZlhtmzZX7kEXynmFZSlXQg-frP2VNvSmpM_Y_NvEJUg1Qo6bRY1vzfSfRElgNHrui_YRF0756kfz3VuU4Le_mAvn4QN8Wgd6EVSbcgOrQrSO?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

Why is this process NOT ideal for intricate designs?

options.content: High setup time for each item

questions.specification: Batch production is typically characterized by:

options.content: Highest output with minimal human input

questions.specification: When designing a production system, understanding the components ensures: [1 mark]

A team of design students is researching how different manufacturing processes influence product design and material selection. Their project explores how shaping techniques, part complexity, and tolerances vary across different contexts. The following figures showcase key examples they are evaluating.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** demonstrates a high-volume manufacturing method ideal for producing identical plastic parts.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/G1a7iVP6BSLsvnAJeQFNC)

Identify the manufacturing process used in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme: - Injection moulding <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why injection moulding (the production method identified above) is appropriate for phone case production **(Figure 1**).
parts.markscheme:
- Allows rapid production of thousands of identical units <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tooling cost is offset by high production volume <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports complex shapes and integrated clips or logos <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** illustrates a subtractive manufacturing process commonly used for precision metal components.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/lrgJaelH330yPq9kc-eew)

Outline two advantages of using CNC milling for metal parts as shown in **Figure 2**.
parts.markscheme:
- High precision allows for tight tolerances in machined parts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Digital control enables repeatable production with minimal error <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** depicts a flexible forming technique for low-to-medium volume metal production.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Mg6eMkHvTKRRTSQDqy2tA)

Evaluate the suitability of sand casting (**Figure 3**) for creating customised engine blocks in low volumes.
parts.markscheme:
- Ideal for producing large, complex metal shapes at moderate cost <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sand moulds can be tailored for unique part designs and easily modified <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Tolerances are lower than machined parts, which may affect fit <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Post-processing (e.g., machining) is often needed for key surfaces <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suitable for short runs where making expensive dies is impractical <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Skilled labour may be needed to create moulds and manage defects <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the surface finish and dimensional accuracy between sand casting (**Figure 3**) and CNC milling (**Figure 2**).
parts.markscheme:
- CNC milling produces smoother finishes due to precise tool paths <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sand casting typically results in rougher surfaces from the sand mould <marks>1 mark</marks>
- CNC parts offer higher dimensional accuracy and tighter tolerances <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cast parts may shrink or deform slightly during cooling <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how the choice between additive and subtractive manufacturing might affect material usage and waste.
parts.markscheme:
- Additive processes (e.g., 3D printing) build up material with minimal waste <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Subtractive methods (e.g., CNC milling) remove material, generating excess scrap <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Material-efficient processes can reduce cost and environmental impact <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Additive methods may offer slower speeds or weaker material properties <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
What design limitation is imposed by the manufacturing system shown?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcyDypbxWQOuLLYD99RUF7uWv-SzvlLMr88uBBSBqyY2l5rA3bE80PGMRBPlGtJzOaaIgrcm4VS1S4Rqo1Z1ZckGf3TzaN6tE2Hz5o0qXShDZajB2ej-i22ToeUZ6hL8-A7JXg4Qw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Designs must be simplified to 2D cuts
options.markscheme: The image shows a vinyl cutter (plotter), which is a CNC manufacturing system that operates by moving a blade or tool along two axes (X and Y). Therefore, it is limited to cutting 2D profiles from flat sheet materials.
While some optical sensors on such machines might be affected by color, the primary design limitation of the mechanical system itself is the dimensionality of the tool path, not the material color.
CNC cutting systems can accurately reproduce both geometric and organic shapes as long as they are 2D vectors.

The manufacturing process itself is automated. While the final product may require assembly (e.g., 'weeding' vinyl), this is a characteristic of the medium/product rather than a limitation imposed by the machinery's design capability.

questions.specification: For complex geometries and high tolerances, manufacturers are most likely to select:
A design must account for the fact that a given production system:

**Type:** MCQ | **Level:** HL | **Paper:** Paper 1

Why is this production method ideal for the product shown?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfLbJivO9M7oEdZx5korkAt-N7kNfsskxzO9EUI5zpSVCM1J2yGDz670iOyto1LUouiQGJDmYMm-t_9EnHPiyocvQcgvxG0zrbIZRPLbDMDwJCiXXQD4J9jAydonhKAn2vI3mYu7g?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: Full automation is a characteristic of mass or continuous production, whereas slip casting (shown) can be manual or semi-automated.
Moulds produce consistent, repeated shapes rather than unique custom shapes for individual units.

The image shows slip casting, a batch production method. Batch production is ideal for moderate demand where multiple identical items are produced, offering flexibility for variety in finishes or glazes.
Unique designs are characteristic of one-off production, while the use of moulds facilitates identical repetition within a batch.

parts.content: Explain how cost and environmental considerations affect the selection of a production system.
parts.markscheme:
- Low-cost systems may favour mechanisation or basic automation, as these require lower initial capital investment than fully automated systems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- High-cost systems with potential for long-term savings may opt for Computer Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) to maximize efficiency and reduce unit costs over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sustainable production systems favour the use of low-emission processes or recyclable materials to minimize environmental footprint <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy-efficient production systems are often selected to reduce long-term operational costs and carbon emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lifecycle cost analysis is used to determine system choice by evaluating the total cost of ownership from setup to decommissioning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Green certification or environmental regulations often mandate the selection of specific production systems that meet compliance standards <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each distinct, well-explained factor up to [6 max]. Ensure both cost and environmental considerations are addressed for full marks.</note>

questions.specification:
What type of production system is likely used to manufacture these identical objects?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXekD133T1HRyXp8amdCzy4wCzKObAb9NQQdnASjS-QBYm4pGRMdi-MCI5FCh7YGOkc6SLL5-xwK9jPSKRoPSGtCdf0llDQS1qWyzWDA1kz5vPR6wpl8RQFa9T8JYYGDyX5O4q2gfA?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

parts.content: Define the term "computer integrated manufacturing (CIM)".
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that CIM is a system that uses computers to control the entire production process <marks>1 mark</marks>

- **Explain** that it integrates design, planning, and manufacturing functions through digital networks <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for identifying CIM and [1] for explaining its integration across production processes.</note>

questions.specification: Which production system is likely to offer the greatest economies of scale?
A company producing bottled water globally would most likely use:

options.markscheme: Job production involves creating unique, custom-made items, which is not appropriate for high-volume, standardized products like bottled water.

Flow production (often referred to as mass production) involves the continuous production of standardized products in high volumes. This method allows global bottled water companies to achieve significant economies of scale and maintain consistent product quality.

Batch production involves making a group of identical products at once. While it offers some flexibility, it is less efficient than flow production for the continuous, high-volume scale required by global water brands.

Cellular manufacturing involves organizing production into self-contained units (cells). While it improves productivity and teamwork, it is typically used for more complex assemblies rather than simple, highly standardized processes like bottling water.
